title: Catechist's Guide — The Paschal Mystery
html:
Catechist's Guide

For preparing and leading the class. For catechist use only.

Class Summary

Estimated duration: 70-75 minutes (this class is dense; don't rush it)

Central theme: The Paschal Mystery of Christ —Passion, Death, Descent into Hell, Resurrection, and Ascension— is the absolutely irreducible center of Christian faith and of all the sacraments.

Objectives:

  • Understand the five moments of the Paschal Mystery as one saving action of God.
  • Recognize the Resurrection as a real historical fact, not a metaphor or myth.
  • Know the historical evidence for the Resurrection and be able to present it.
  • Understand the Ascension as our humanity's entrance into heaven.
  • Connect the Paschal Mystery with the liturgy (anamnesis) and Baptism.
  • Be moved by Holy Week as an annual immersion in the Paschal Mystery.

Key verse: "If Christ has not been raised, our preaching is in vain and your faith is in vain. But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep" (1 Cor 15:14,20)

Catechism references: CCC 1084-1090; 571-655

Suggested Class Structure

Section Duration What the catechist does
1. The Great Question: the empty tomb 10 min Narrate the scene of the women at dawn (Lk 24:1-8). Read aloud, without rushing. Provocative question: "If this is true, what changes in the universe? If it's false, what's left for us?"
2. The five moments of the Paschal Mystery 12 min Explain each moment with its theological significance. Draw a timeline or circle on the board. Make sure to clarify the Descent into Hell (CCC 633): not the hell of the damned but the abode of the dead.
3. Historical evidence for the Resurrection 12 min Present the seven lines of evidence. You can distribute one piece of evidence to small groups for discussion. Read 1 Cor 15:14 aloud.
4. Consequences for our lives 8 min The five consequences of the Resurrection. Focus especially on the fourth and fifth (we already live risen; we are resurrected). Read Rom 6:3-5. Connect with Baptism.
5. The Triduum and the Easter Vigil 8 min Explain what the Triduum is. Narrate the Easter Vigil in detail: darkness, the Paschal Candle, the Exsultet, readings, baptisms. Show photos or a video of a Vigil if you have them. Invite everyone to attend this year.
6. Scriptural Connection: the kerygma 7 min Read 1 Cor 15:3-8 and Rom 6:4-5 aloud. Explain that 1 Cor 15 contains a creed older than the Gospels. The faith has solid historical roots.
7. For Reflection 10 min Choose two or three questions from the material. Pair work, then share with the whole group (only those who want to). The catechist does not judge answers.
8. Closing: Key Idea + Prayer 5 min Read the Key Idea. Pray the class prayer. Hand out or assign the workbook. Remind them: "If the Resurrection is not true, nothing matters. If it is true, nothing else matters."

Notes for the Catechist

This class is different

You're not teaching one more sacrament. You're teaching the foundation of all sacraments. If the group takes away nothing else from the entire course except this, the course will have been worth it. Don't rush. Give yourself permission to spend more time if the group engages. The Passion, Resurrection, and Ascension are not topics: they are THE topic.

The historical evidence matters

Many Catholics grew up believing that the Resurrection is something "accepted by faith" without any rational basis. This is a grave pastoral error. Faith is reasonable. The apostles did not ask anyone to believe without evidence: they gave evidence —living witnesses, the empty tomb, visible transformation— and invited people to verify. Present it this way: God does not ask us for a blind leap into the void. He asks us for a reasonable step toward the light.

The C.S. Lewis trilemma

It is one of the most effective arguments with young people and skeptics. Jesus claimed to be God, he claimed he would rise, and he claimed to forgive sins (something only God can do). Faced with someone who makes such assertions, there are only three options: he is a lunatic (delusions of grandeur), a liar (deliberate deception), or he is who he claims to be (God incarnate). No "great moral teacher but mistaken about himself" option exists: a moral teacher who thinks he's God is not a great moral teacher. Develop it in your own words.

The descent into hell: clarify terminology

This point often causes confusion. The "hell" in the Creed translates the Latin inferos, not infernum. Christ went to the sheol, the abode of the dead, to liberate the just, not to the hell of the damned. The ancient Holy Saturday homily cited in CCC 635 is stunningly beautiful.

The Easter Vigil: it's not optional

Ask the group: how many have ever attended a full Easter Vigil? Normally less than half raise their hands. Insist: the Vigil is not a "longer Mass." It is the foundational liturgy of Christianity. If someone says "it's too long," respond: "Christ spent three hours on the cross. Three hours of Vigil isn't that much, is it?"

The Shroud of Turin

Mention the Shroud of Turin as an interesting fact, not as an argument for the faith. The Church does not require belief in its authenticity. But science has not been able to explain how it was formed. If a student mentions the 1988 carbon-14 dating (which gave a medieval date), clarify that the sample was taken from a corner mended in the 14th century, as confirmed by later studies (Ray Rogers, 2005). The Shroud remains a scientific enigma. But again: it's not doctrine. Don't base your class on this.

Adaptation by Age

🌱 Seed (children 7-12)

Tell the story as a true tale. Sunday morning, the sad women, the angel, the joy. Activities: draw the empty tomb (the angel pointing to the rolled-away stone, the women surprised). Color a Paschal Candle. Make a card saying "Jesus is risen, alleluia!" to give to someone. Memorize the phrase: "He is not here; he has risen." No forensic details about the scourging; focus on the joy and the triumph of God's love over death.

🌿 Shoots (youth 13-17)

Start with the C.S. Lewis trilemma. Skepticism hits hard at this age; give them arguments. The historical evidence will appeal to them: the witnesses, the empty tomb, the transformation of the apostles. Ask them directly: "What do you think? Did the disciples invent the Resurrection, or did it really happen?" Let them debate. Use a clip from Risen (2016). Connect with their experience: what "deaths" are they living through (failures, breakups, loneliness)? The Resurrection is not just a past event: it is a promise for their own deaths.

🌲 Root (adults new to the faith)

Many adults carry the idea that the Resurrection is a metaphor. Dismantle it with affection and solidity. The Resurrection is the culminating truth of the faith (CCC 638). If it's not true, the Church is a con. Explain that they can believe reasonably. The testimony of the apostles is not naive: it is the testimony of people who died for affirming they had seen a man risen from the dead. Connect with their lives: if Christ rose, their own daily deaths (failures, losses, illnesses) do not have the last word.

🌳 Tree (formed adults)

Deepen into the theology of the risen body (CCC 640-646, 999-1001). The distinction between the corruptible body and the glorified body (soma pneumatikon vs soma psychikon in 1 Cor 15:44). The resurrection of the flesh as a dogma the ancient world rejected (Paul at the Areopagus, Acts 17:32). Relate it to the theology of the sacraments as an anticipation of the final resurrection. Discuss the relationship between the Eucharist and the Resurrection. Cite St. Thomas Aquinas, Summa Theologiae III, q. 53-56. Reference Benedict XVI's Jesus of Nazareth II.

Additional Resources

  • YouCat: Questions 97-112 (Jesus Christ: Passion, Death, and Resurrection).
  • Compendium of the Catechism: Questions 112-133.
  • Benedict XVI: Jesus of Nazareth II, chapter 9, "The Resurrection of Jesus from the Dead." Required reading for the catechist before teaching this class.
  • C.S. Lewis: Mere Christianity, Book II, chapter 3, "The Shocking Alternative." Brief (10 pages), accessible, devastating.
  • Video: Clips from Risen (2016), especially the investigation of the empty tomb.
  • Music: Victimae Paschali Laudes (Easter sequence). Exsultet (Easter Proclamation in Gregorian chant).
  • Document: The Paschal Mystery in the Sacraments, series of catecheses by St. John Paul II (1987-1988).

Difficult Questions

"Isn't it easier to think the disciples had hallucinations or made the story up?"

The hallucination hypothesis does not withstand analysis. Hallucinations are individual phenomena, not collective. Five hundred people do not have the same hallucination at the same time. Moreover, hallucinations do not eat fish or let you touch their wounds. And most importantly: a hallucination does not convert a persecutor into a martyr. Paul was not expecting to see the risen Jesus; he was actively persecuting his followers. His conversion is one of the best-documented facts of antiquity, and the hallucination hypothesis does not explain it. As for fabrication: people do not voluntarily die for something they know is false. The apostles were in a position to know whether Jesus had risen or not. And they gave their lives for that testimony.

"How can someone physically rise from the dead? It violates the laws of nature."

Precisely: a dead person does not rise by natural causes. The Resurrection is not a natural phenomenon but a direct intervention of the Creator. God, who created the universe and its laws, can act above them. The Resurrection is not a violation of nature: it is a transfiguration of nature from within by the power of God. It is not that matter has made a mistake; it is that matter has been elevated to a new condition.

"If Jesus rose, why didn't he appear to everyone? Why only to his followers?"

The risen Jesus did not appear as a public spectacle because faith is proposed, not imposed. If Christ had appeared in the Temple before the Sanhedrin, the freedom to believe or not believe would have been nullified. God respects our freedom so deeply that even after rising, he forces no one to believe. He appears to those he loves and whom he chooses as witnesses, and through them, he invites everyone to believe. But he always leaves room for freedom. Faith is not an obligatory mathematical calculation: it is a free act of trust in the testimony of credible witnesses.

"Don't the Gospels contradict each other in their Resurrection accounts?"

There are differences in details (who went first to the tomb, how many angels there were, etc.), but no contradictions about the central facts: the empty tomb, the appearances of Jesus, the initial disbelief of the disciples, the mission to announce. In fact, the small differences in secondary details strengthen historical credibility: if the four evangelists had conspired to invent a story, they would have made everything match perfectly. Minor variations are typical of independent accounts of the same real event.

"What about people who have never heard of the Resurrection? Are they condemned?"

The Church clearly teaches that God desires all people to be saved (1 Tim 2:4). Those who, through no fault of their own, do not know Christ but sincerely seek God and strive to do his will according to the dictates of their conscience, can attain salvation by paths known only to God (CCC 847, cf. Lumen Gentium 16). Christ's Resurrection is the sole source of all salvation, but the fruits of that Resurrection can reach those who do not explicitly know it, if they live according to the truth they have.

Catechist's Prayer

Lord Jesus, Risen One,
who conquered death and opened the gates of heaven,
give me words that do not slide off but pierce through.
May those who hear me today not hear just another class,
but the news that changed the world:
that the tomb is empty and You are alive.
Remove from my heart all routine, all haste,
all fear of speaking what truly matters.
Let me speak not as one repeating a lesson,
but as one who has seen the Risen Lord
and can no longer keep silent.
Amen.

Print Catechist's Guide
